source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Explain that unsupported objects fall towards the Earth because of gravity; identify the effects of air resistance, water resistance and friction between moving surfaces; recognise that levers, pulleys and gears allow a smaller force to have a greater effect.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack develops force-effect reasoning through controlled virtual comparisons, force-arrow models and mechanism trade-offs, while keeping mass, weight, speed and force conceptually distinct.

objective.child_goal: I can use evidence and force models to explain falling, resistance, friction and mechanisms.
objective.statement: Explain effects of gravity, air resistance, water resistance and friction, and use models to reason about levers, pulleys and gears.
misconceptions: Thinks heavier objects always fall faster because gravity pulls only on heavy objects.
Treats air resistance, water resistance or friction as forces that always stop movement rather than forces that affect motion.
Changes several variables in a comparison and then attributes the outcome to one chosen variable.
Thinks a mechanism creates free force with no trade-off in distance, direction or number of movements.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A force that pulls objects with mass towards one another; near Earth it pulls objects towards Earth.
vocabulary.audio_script: Gravity pulls unsupported objects towards the Earth.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A downward force arrow from an object labelled gravity.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A force that acts against motion through air, water or between surfaces.
vocabulary.audio_script: Resistance acts in the opposite direction to movement.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Motion and resistance arrows pointing in opposite directions.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A force between surfaces that are moving or trying to move across each other.
vocabulary.audio_script: Friction can slow movement, provide grip or produce heating.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two touching surfaces with an opposing force arrow.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A set of moving parts that changes a force or movement.
vocabulary.audio_script: A mechanism can change force size, direction, speed or distance.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Lever, pulley and gear icons around an input-output diagram.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Something that can be changed, measured or kept the same in an investigation.
vocabulary.audio_script: Change one variable, measure the effect and keep important conditions the same.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A fair-test panel with CHANGE, MEASURE and KEEP SAME columns.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: For each example, identify the movement change: start, stop, speed up, slow down or change direction.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Activate observable force effects before naming specific forces.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A force is inferred from what changes in an object's motion or shape.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Before-and-after motion cards show direction and speed without requiring animation.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Use arrows to show which forces act and which way they act.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce qualitative force-arrow models as explanations, not decorative labels.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Arrow direction shows the direction of the force. Compare arrows only within the model's stated scale.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: An object sits between labelled motion and force-arrow layers with a model-not-to-scale note.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Two equal masses fall with different canopy areas. The larger canopy produces more air resistance and falls more slowly.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model a controlled comparison and explain an outcome using gravity and air resistance.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Mass and drop height stay the same. Canopy area changes. The larger area increases air resistance.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Side-by-side parachutes share mass and height labels, while area and fall-time data differ.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Choose one variable, predict the effect, run repeated trials and explain the pattern in the data.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Coordinate fair-test planning, simulation evidence and causal explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Change one factor, keep the comparison fair and use repeated results rather than one dramatic trial.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A simulator links CHANGE-MEASURE-KEEP SAME controls to a data table and force model.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: A flat paper sheet falls more slowly than the same sheet crumpled. Does this prove the crumpled paper has more gravity?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Distinguish gravity from differing air resistance and challenge heavier-falls-faster reasoning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The mass is the same. Shape changes the air resistance, so the falling motion changes.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Equal-mass labels remain fixed while area and resistance arrows change.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion how a lever can reduce the input force without giving free work.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Explain force multiplication alongside the increased distance moved and pivot position.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A smaller force acts over a greater distance. Show the input, pivot, load and movement trade-off.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A lever model compares two pivot positions with input force and distance readouts.

manipulatives.purpose: Compare gravity, air resistance, water resistance and friction through controlled virtual investigations and qualitative force models.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners select a scenario, change one permitted variable, run repeated trials, inspect a data table and attach force arrows to explain the observed motion.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: All motion has step-frame and data-table equivalents; controls use labelled sliders plus number inputs; no flashing or sudden impacts occur; force direction is announced; graphs duplicate values in tables; and the simulation can run at quarter speed or no-motion mode.

manipulatives.purpose: Explore how lever pivot position, pulley arrangement and gear size alter force, direction, speed or movement distance.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners adjust one mechanism feature, predict input/output effects and compare force and distance indicators before explaining the trade-off.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Every configuration has a labelled static diagram, keyboard step controls, tactile-style high-contrast symbols, spoken input/output summaries and a no-drag component chooser; direction is shown with words and arrows, never colour alone.

practice.interleaving_notes: Establish force direction and fair comparisons before mixing resistance types; introduce mechanisms as input-output systems after learners can separate observed motion, measured data and force explanation.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain falling towards Earth and distinguish gravity effects from air-resistance differences.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: paired falling objects + controlled mass/shape/area variables + step frames + qualitative force arrows + explanation choice
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid claims about ideal vacuum behaviour beyond the evidence shown; distinguish mass from weight and do not imply gravity is absent when an object is supported.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Plan and interpret controlled comparisons of shape, area or speed in air and water.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: scenario + choose change/measure/keep-same variables + prediction + repeated timing data + cautious conclusion
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Virtual contexts must be safe and physically plausible; conclusions should match the tested range rather than claim universal rules.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain friction as an opposing contact force that can slow movement, provide grip or cause heating.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: surface pair + motion task + force direction + observation/data + beneficial/harmful effect explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use age-appropriate everyday examples and avoid unsafe instructions involving burns, roads or uncontrolled sliding.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Reason qualitatively about how mechanisms change force, direction, speed or distance.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: lever/pulley/gear diagram + adjustable feature + input/output prediction + force-distance or speed-direction comparison
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not present exact mechanical advantage unless the model supplies sufficient quantities; always expose the trade-off or changed direction.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain force vocabulary, fair-test reasoning and model limitations through short mixed review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review queue + static scenario/data snippet + identify force/effect/evidence/model limit
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use static presentation by default and ensure reading complexity does not exceed the scientific reasoning demand.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Read and critique qualitative force-arrow diagrams for supported, falling and sliding objects.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: static object/motion state + labelled arrows + direction/balance/model-limit choice
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Arrow lengths must be described as qualitative unless measurements justify magnitude.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use data and force models to make cautious predictions, explain evidence and transfer ideas to new contexts.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: scenario/table/model + prediction or transfer claim + evidence and limitation explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Conclusions must stay within tested ranges and state limitations when transferring from simulations.

body.prompt: The same sheet of paper is dropped flat and then crumpled from the same height. Why does the crumpled paper usually reach the ground first?
body.changed: shape and area facing the air
choices: The flat sheet experiences more air resistance
The crumpled sheet has gained more gravity
Gravity stops acting on the flat sheet
The flat sheet has no mass
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: The flat sheet experiences more air resistance

hints: The same paper has the same mass in both trials.
Compare the area pushing through the air.
question_variants.explanation: Both forms are pulled towards Earth by gravity, but the flat sheet has a larger area and experiences more air resistance, so it falls more slowly.

feedback.correct: Correct. Both forms are pulled towards Earth by gravity, but the flat sheet has a larger area and experiences more air resistance, so it falls more slowly.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: The same paper has the same mass in both trials. Compare the area pushing through the air. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the heavy always falls faster route: The same paper has the same mass in both trials. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the heavy always falls faster idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: Plan a fair test of how parachute canopy area affects fall time.
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

hints: Change only the factor named in the question.
Measure the outcome and keep other important conditions the same.
question_variants.explanation: A fair comparison changes canopy area, measures fall time and keeps mass, drop height and material the same across repeated trials.

feedback.correct: Correct. A fair comparison changes canopy area, measures fall time and keeps mass, drop height and material the same across repeated trials.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: Change only the factor named in the question. Measure the outcome and keep other important conditions the same. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the multiple variables changed route: Change only the factor named in the question. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the multiple variables changed idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: A shoe with a textured sole slips less than a smooth sole on the same dry surface. Which explanation best fits?
observations: textured sole requires more pull before sliding
choices: Greater friction provides more grip between the textured sole and the surface
Friction has disappeared from the textured sole
The textured sole makes gravity switch off
Smooth surfaces always create the greatest friction
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: Greater friction provides more grip between the textured sole and the surface

hints: The textured sole needs more force before it begins to slide.
Friction can provide useful grip as well as slow movement.
question_variants.explanation: The evidence suggests greater friction between the textured sole and the dry surface, providing more grip and resisting sliding.

feedback.correct: Correct. The evidence suggests greater friction between the textured sole and the dry surface, providing more grip and resisting sliding.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: The textured sole needs more force before it begins to slide. Friction can provide useful grip as well as slow movement. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the friction only bad route: The textured sole needs more force before it begins to slide. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the friction only bad idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: A learner moves a lever's pivot closer to the load. What is the likely effect at the long input end?
choices: A smaller input force can lift the load, but the input end moves farther
The lever creates energy, so no input movement is needed
The load becomes massless
The input force and movement distance must both become zero
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: A smaller input force can lift the load, but the input end moves farther

hints: Compare the pivot-to-load distance with the pivot-to-input distance.
A force advantage comes with a movement-distance trade-off.
question_variants.explanation: Placing the pivot closer to the load gives the input side a longer lever arm, so a smaller input force can work, but the input end must move through a greater distance.

feedback.correct: Correct. Placing the pivot closer to the load gives the input side a longer lever arm, so a smaller input force can work, but the input end must move through a greater distance.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: Compare the pivot-to-load distance with the pivot-to-input distance. A force advantage comes with a movement-distance trade-off. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the mechanism free force route: Compare the pivot-to-load distance with the pivot-to-input distance. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the mechanism free force idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: Sky Motion Laboratory investigation 1: A ball is released from a hand. Why does it begin moving towards the ground?
choices: The air pushes it down because all air moves downward.
The ball has used up its support force.
Gravity starts only after the ball moves.
Gravity pulls the unsupported ball towards Earth.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks heavier objects always fall faster because gravity pulls only on heavy objects.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner explains a falling-time difference only by mass or says gravity is absent from a light or slowly falling object.
repair_sequence: Label gravity acting towards Earth on both objects.
Compare same-mass shapes or matched-shape objects while identifying air resistance separately.
Use the controlled evidence to explain the observed fall without a blanket heavier-is-faster rule.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner explains a new falling comparison using both gravity and relevant resistance evidence.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats air resistance, water resistance or friction as forces that always stop movement rather than forces that affect motion.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner says motion cannot continue when resistance is present or describes friction only as harmful.
repair_sequence: Show motion and opposing resistance arrows together.
Compare cases where the object continues, slows or gains useful grip.
Describe the effect on motion without claiming the resistance must stop it.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner identifies an opposing force and gives one slowing effect and one useful effect in new contexts.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Changes several variables in a comparison and then attributes the outcome to one chosen variable.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The plan changes shape, mass or height together, or the conclusion names a cause not isolated by the investigation.
repair_sequence: Sort variables into CHANGE, MEASURE and KEEP SAME.
Lock all but one changed variable in the simulator.
Run repeated trials and limit the conclusion to the tested comparison.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner plans a fresh controlled comparison and writes a conclusion supported by the repeated data.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks a mechanism creates free force with no trade-off in distance, direction or number of movements.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner predicts a smaller input force with unchanged input distance and no other system change.
repair_sequence: Mark input point, output point, pivot or axle.
Compare force and movement distance before and after the configuration change.
State the advantage and the accompanying trade-off or direction change.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner explains a new lever, pulley or gear model using both an input advantage and an output trade-off.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm virtual lab with muted backgrounds, no collision sounds, no rapid drops and one active force model at a time.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace falling, flowing and rotating animations with numbered still frames, direction arrows and complete data tables.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Where listening materially supports motion sequencing, force-arrow descriptions or mechanism input/output comparison, optional playback uses only ElevenLabs assets after human listening approval. Browser TTS is prohibited; every task remains complete through static frames, text descriptions, tables and adult or partner reading.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use short investigation cards, icons for change/measure/keep same, glossary popovers and force-arrow descriptions that do not require interpreting dense diagrams.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Lock irrelevant controls, display PREDICT-TEST-RECORD-EXPLAIN progress and pause automatically after each trial for observation.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Remove timers, provide quarter-speed and no-motion modes, preserve trial data, allow repeated runs and expose graph values in a sortable table.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat predictions as testable ideas, preserve correct variable choices during repair and reward updating an explanation when evidence changes.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach force, gravity, resistance, friction, surface, variable, input and output with gestures, paired arrows and sentence frames.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires correct force-effect explanations, a fair resistance test and a qualitative mechanism model in at least two formats. Secure requires evidence-based comparison, controlled-variable reasoning and explanation of a mechanism trade-off on two retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can explain how gravity, resistance and friction affect movement and how simple mechanisms change forces.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Separates prediction, variable plan, observations, data and force-model explanation so scientific reasoning can be assessed independently of reading speed or fine-motor control.
next_actions: Route heavier-falls-faster claims to same-mass shape comparisons.
Route causal overclaims to CHANGE-MEASURE-KEEP SAME repair.
Extend secure mechanism reasoning to linked gears and pulley systems.
Schedule mixed force, fair-test and mechanism retrieval at 1, 3, 7, 14 and 30 days.

qa.notes: Third production-wave review bank reaches the 240-item pilot target with four preserved curated questions and 236 deterministic candidates covering gravity, air and water resistance, friction, levers/pulleys/gears, variables and fair tests, force diagrams/models, evidence, prediction, misconception repair and transfer. Generated candidates include SEND-accessible simulation, visual, text and concrete alternatives, supported non-drag interactions, rich corrective feedback and pressure-free investigation missions. Optional ElevenLabs references are limited to motion, force-model and mechanism comparisons, require human listening approval, and never use browser TTS. Human science, teacher, SEND, accessibility, safeguarding, audio and renderer review remains required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
